Press enter or click to view image in full size

6 min read
Just now

--

There’s a particular flavor of thinking that has started to drive me a little insane lately, and it’s not just online, I’m seeing it inside real companies too.
It usually goes something like a founder or a CTO posts a video of Claude or some agent swarm spinning up a project, and the caption says something like “I built what used to take a team 6 months in 16 hours.” The comments fill up with fire emojis. Someone says we are entering a new era. Someone else says their engineering team is now obsolete.
Then there is a 100x engineer running ten sub agents in parallel. Btw why even stop at 100x? just spawn a thousand and call yourself a 1000x engineer. But what are you even building at this point? Do you know, or did you just watch a terminal scroll really fast and assume something good was happening inside it?
And yes, I get it. A working MVP in two days is genuinely impressive, Claude did real heavy lifting for you. But it’s not always the way it looks. Every time I see one of these posts I just wanna ask that do you actually know what you built and how it works? Or did you just tell Claude “yo, build me a multi-million dollar product, pick the features yourself, figure it all out” and wait for the terminal to stop scrolling?
And I’m not only talking about strangers on the internet. I’ve heard a version of this exact line said out loud in my own workplace, by someone who genuinely believed a project that took a team 6 months could be completed by Claude in 16 hours. Once you’ve heard it said by someone in a position to actually make that call for your team, the LinkedIn posts stop feeling like harmless hype and start feeling a lot more dangerous.
The 6 months was never about typing code
When a system takes atleast 6 months to build, the writing of the code is a real part of that timeline, but it’s nowhere near the whole thing. What actually eats most of that timeline is everything around the code. Figuring out what the product should even do. Arguing about whether feature X is worth the complexity it adds. Realizing halfway through that your whole data model is wrong because someone changed a requirement. Going back and forth with users, trying to figure out what they actually need, which is usually nothing like what they told you they need. Making a hundred tiny tradeoffs between speed, cost, and how much of a mess you’ll be cleaning up later, and every one of those quietly decides how the next few months go.
AI surely collapses the coding part, But that was not the whole story. Spawning more sub agents does not speed up a debate about whether the product should even have this feature or any other technical decision. You sped up the part that was always gonna get done anyway. The real bottleneck? Still sitting right where it always was. The thinking, the deciding, the endless back and forth in meetings.
So when someone says “I built in 16 hours what took 6 months,” what they actually mean is someone already made every hard decision for them and handed over a finished spec. That’s a completely different thing and software engineering doesn’t usually work like that. It’s like timing how fast a crew can frame a house from a finished blueprint, against the months the architect spent figuring out how people would actually live in it, what it had to withstand, and whether it should be built there at all.
Vibe coding and the two day miracle that becomes a two month nightmare
I’ve seen this pattern play out enough times even at my workplace now. Someone spins up an MVP on Claude in two days. An expense tracker, a small internal tool, whatever. It looks slick, it demos well, everyone in the room is amazed. The CEO is thrilled. The investors are thrilled.
Then real usage starts. Someone tries to add a feature and three other things quietly break. Edge cases show up that nobody thought about, the AI just wrote whatever got the demo working. There’s no real architecture, no one made a decision about how the data should be structured for the long run. And here’s the part that matters, that’s not the tool failing. Claude will happily explain every choice it made if you ask. The problem is nobody asked. Nobody read the diffs. It was a black box from day one, not because the AI is a black box, but because the person using it never looked inside.
So what happens next? The CEO assigns an actual engineering team. That team doesn’t get to build something new. They spend the next two or three weeks to months untangling a system nobody, including the original builder, fully understands. They’re doing archaeology on code that has no decisions written down anywhere, just output. Congrats on the 16 hour build, here’s your weeks (or months) of cleanup.
The flood nobody asked for
This is the part that actually worries me more than the hype itself, and the numbers are finally catching up to it.
In early 2026, submissions to Apple’s App Store jumped 84% in a single quarter, 235,800 new apps and reporters pinned the surge directly on vibe coding. It was the biggest jump in roughly a decade.
The barrier to building collapsed, so people built. Nobody stopped to ask whether half of it should even exist. Good products used to get built slowly, one piece at a time, where every feature had to earn its place. Someone in the room would actually push back and go “wait, does this really add anything, or are we just building it because we can?” That friction was a feature. It was the thing standing between a product that solved a real problem and a product that was just technically functional and proud of it. Speed without that friction doesn’t give you more good products. It gives you more noise wearing a launch announcement.
The returns that never showed up
it is only logical to think that companies having the “100x” engineers would be making at least 10x profits, right? but obviously that’s not the case the way it looks so far. In PwC’s 2026 Global CEO Survey of 4,454 chief executives across 95 countries, 56% said AI had delivered no significant financial benefit at all, and only 12% said it had both grown revenue and cut costs. These are the people who signed the checks, admitting the money mostly didn’t come back. And the ones who did see returns had one thing in common, they’d built the discipline and the foundations around the tool first. Which is the same point this whole post keeps circling back to. The tool worked. The thinking around it didn’t.
So how do you actually get better at this
There’s no trick, and it’s the opposite of spawning a thousand agents and hoping a Jira clone falls out the other end. The work that always mattered still matters. Deciding what to build, what it needs to hold up against, whether it should even exist. What changes is your role. You go from writing every line yourself to making sure the right calls get made before anyone writes anything at all.
Use the speed where it belongs. The boilerplate, the scaffolding, the stuff that was never where the real value lived. And keep the judgment for yourself. Read what the agent writes, and understand why it made the choices it made. If you can’t explain how your own product works, you don’t own it, you’re just hosting it and hoping. And stay suspicious of anything that came together in two days and feels too good and ask what happens to it in three months of real usage, not what happens to it on stage.
Good things still take time. Spawn all the sub agents you want but the meeting about whether this should exist still has to happen, and no amount of agents are showing up to that one for you.
